one_line_takeaway: CachePrune defends against indirect prompt injection by attributing and pruning 'instruction-following' neurons in the context KV cache, sharply reducing attack success rate with no test-time overhead and negligible utility loss - though only benchmarked against prompt-engineering baselines on 7-8B models in templated QA/summarization settings.
key_finding: CachePrune substantially lowers ASR while preserving clean-instruction utility. On LLaMA3-8B/SQuAD it cuts ASR from 27.86 (Vanilla) to 7.44 with F1(clean) held at 28.68 vs 28.20. On LLaMA3-8B/HotpotQA it drops ASR from 69.01 to 15.23. On Mistral-7B/SQuAD ASR falls from 9.01 to 0.68, and on Phi-3.5/HotpotQA from 21.67 to 1.76. The only baseline matching its ASR is Encode_Base64, but that comes at severe utility cost (e.g., LLaMA3/SQuAD F1(clean) collapses to 13.34 vs CachePrune's 28.68). Ablations show early tokens matter most (k=1 gives ASR 7.44 vs L_attr_full's 14.81), pruning must be restricted to Phi at higher prune ratios (p=5%: with Phi F1(clean) 27.48 vs without Phi 6.48), pruned neurons concentrate in middle layers and are more often key than value neurons, and masks transfer across attack types and instructions.
core_contribution: CachePrune is a test-time defense against indirect prompt injection (IPI) that edits the prompt context's KV cache. Its own novel mechanism has two stages: (1) Neural Attribution, which localizes neurons in the context KV cache whose activations bias the LLM toward treating injected context text as instructions to follow rather than as data, using an attribution score a_t^i = h_t^i * dL_attr/dh_t^i; and (2) Intervention, which prunes those neurons by zeroing their rows in the context KV cache via a learned binary mask, so the context is interpreted as pure data. The paper's own contributions beyond prior neuron-attribution work are: a 'preferential attribution loss' L_attr that measures how much the model favors a poisoned response over a clean one (the difference of greedy poisoned vs. clean response probabilities), which it theoretically ties to an upper bound of the DPO objective (Theorem 1, Lemma 1); a regularization subset Phi that excludes neurons important to clean responses so pruning does not degrade utility; and exploitation of an observed 'triggering effect' whereby only the first k tokens (default k=1) of the response suffice to distinguish clean vs. poisoned behavior, improving attribution fidelity with as few as N=8 samples. Because pruning is applied when encoding the context KV cache, the defense adds no test-time generation overhead and does not alter prompt formatting.
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection: a third party injects adversarial or unauthorized instructions into the prompt CONTEXT (e.g., retrieved documents, tool outputs) of an LLM-integrated application. The attack succeeds when the model follows the injected instruction instead of the user's actual instruction, producing a 'poisoned' response. The paper assumes the context boundary (start/end token indices c_s, c_e) is known, which it justifies as consistent with LLM-integrated API templates that combine user queries with third-party data (a common assumption in StruQ, Jatmo, etc.). The attacker controls injected text placed at the beginning, middle, or end of the context. Success is measured by Attack Success Rate (ASR); the defense must also preserve the model's ability to answer the legitimate user instruction.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitation is that they do not explore training-based defenses (e.g., adversarial fine-tuning), targeting the low-compute regime, and suggest future comparative study of test-time vs training-time defenses. Additional reviewer-noted constraints grounded in the text: (1) the method requires knowing the context span boundary, limiting it to templated LLM-integrated setups; (2) evaluation is confined to QA and dialogue-summarization tasks on 7-8B-scale open models, not agentic/tool-use settings or large proprietary models; (3) baselines are deliberately restricted to prompt-engineering defenses (Vanilla, Delimiting, Datamarking, Sandwich, Encode_Base64) - no fine-tuning or test-time-workflow defenses are compared head-to-head, so the ASR/utility advantage is only established against lightweight baselines; (4) the theoretical DPO connection is asymptotic (Lemma 1 holds in the limit and the paper notes it no longer holds with few samples); (5) alpha and p introduce a robustness-utility tradeoff that must be tuned.

datasets_used: SQuAD (extractive QA, with injected instructions at begin/middle/end per Abdelnabi et al. 2024 splits)
WildChat (dialogue summarization; model attacked if it answers an in-dialogue question instead of summarizing)
quantitative_results: Encode_Base64 matches ASR but destroys utility: LLaMA3/SQuAD F1(clean) 13.34 vs CachePrune 28.68
Ablation on k (LLaMA3/SQuAD): k=1 ASR 7.44, k=2 ASR 5.57, k=4 ASR 10.77, full-response L_attr ASR 14.81
Ablation on Phi at p=5% (LLaMA3/SQuAD): with Phi ASR 0.83 / F1(clean) 27.48; without Phi ASR 7.86 / F1(clean) 6.48
Ablation on alpha (LLaMA3/SQuAD): alpha=1.5 ASR 6.40, alpha=1.0 ASR 7.44, alpha=0.5 ASR 10.77, alpha=0.3 ASR 13.50
Cross-type mask transfer (SQuAD): Text->Code ASR 3.20 (vs Vanilla Code 17.5); Code->Text ASR 16.9 (vs Vanilla Text 45.15)
Default hyperparameters: N=8 attribution samples, p=0.5% neurons pruned, k=1, alpha=1, 3 trials
baselines_compared: Vanilla (no defense; CachePrune is implemented on top of it)
Datamarking (replace every space in context with a special character)
Sandwich (wrap context with user instruction as a sandwich)
Encode_Base64 (context encoded in Base64, other spans plain text)
